Index › Investment Performance Appraisal › Module 1: Investment Performance Appraisal

1.04 | Distinguishing Investment Skill from Luck

Module 1: Investment Performance Appraisal1.04 | Distinguishing Investment Skill from Luck

 Learning Outcome

The candidate should be able to:

  • describe the problem of distinguishing skill from luck

An investment manager’s record for any specific time period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and/or bad luck (unanticipated bad developments). One reason that luck should be considered important when appraising investment performance is the paradox of skill. As investment managers in aggregate become more experienced, overall “skill” levels are enhanced and the difference between the worst and the best performers is likely to become narrower. Massive computing power and access to “big” data may further narrow investment performance differentials and increase the likelihood that these differentials can be explained by luck.

Determining whether a portfolio manager has or lacks active investment skill based on past returns is difficult and always subject to error. Financial market returns have a large element of randomness. Some of this randomness reflects the impact of news and information that relate directly or indirectly to asset values. Trading motivated by liquidity needs (i.e., to raise money rather than motivated by analysis) and by the sentiments and emotions of investors adds to return volatility.

When the historical performance of an investment portfolio is observed, the record we see is only one out of a potentially unlimited number that the investment manager might have achieved applying the same investment discipline but with different luck. Additional insight into skill can sometimes be gained by examining consistency of performance. But the hypothesis that the manager’s underlying mean return exceeds the benchmark’s mean return may require many years of observations to confirm with a reasonably high degree of confidence.3

3 Can you be lucky once and correctly pick the flip of a fair coin? Of course! How about four times in a row? Yes, although much less likely. Can a portfolio manager be lucky enough to generate 15 continuous years of superior investment performance? Very unlikely, but with hundreds or even thousands of portfolio managers trying, a few might succeed solely because of luck. One problem faced in investment performance appraisal is that many investment management performance records are short, making it difficult to separate luck from skill.

4 1 ‒ (0.95)5 = 1 ‒ 0.774 = 0.226 = 22.6%.
